# /contract-negotiation-prep-brief

Builds the internal prep brief for a vendor contract negotiation - which terms actually matter, where there's real flexibility, where there isn't, and what the vendor is likely to push back on - so the negotiator walks in with a plan instead of winging it deal-point by deal-point.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before any vendor contract negotiation or renewal discussion with real stakes - new vendor contracts, renewals with pricing changes, or any negotiation where multiple terms are in play and the team wants a deliberate strategy rather than reactive back-and-forth.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The draft contract or current terms (for a renewal) and specifically which terms are up for discussion
- The buyer's actual priorities ranked - which terms are must-win, which are nice-to-have, and which are genuinely tradeable, since not all terms carry equal weight
- Any leverage or lack of leverage in this specific negotiation (competing bids in hand, a hard deadline pressuring the buyer, vendor's own incentive to close before their quarter-end) - this materially changes strategy
- Absolute walk-away conditions, if any exist (a term that's non-negotiable for legal/compliance/budget reasons) that must be flagged distinctly from merely-preferred terms

## Safety

- Propose before acting: this is a prep document for the negotiator's internal use; ensure any walk-away point or approval threshold reflects real authority limits (e.g., what dollar amount or term actually requires higher sign-off) rather than assuming the negotiator can commit to anything discussed live.

## Workflow

1. Rank every term in play into three tiers explicitly - must-win, negotiable/tradeable, and nice-to-have - since treating every clause as equally important is how negotiators either fold too easily on something that mattered or burn goodwill fighting over something that didn't.
2. Identify genuine trade opportunities across tiers - e.g., "we can concede on payment terms if we get the liability cap we need," since real negotiation leverage usually comes from trading a low-priority item for a high-priority one, not from just asking for everything.
3. Assess actual leverage honestly before setting expectations - a buyer with competing bids in hand and no urgency has real leverage; a buyer with no alternatives and a hard deadline has much less, and the strategy (and walk-away credibility) should reflect that reality rather than assumed leverage.
4. Anticipate the vendor's likely counter-position on each major ask, based on standard vendor incentives (protecting margin, avoiding liability exposure, preferring auto-renewal terms) - arriving with an anticipated counter already thought through prevents being caught flat-footed.
5. Set a clear walk-away point distinct from a mere preference - the specific term or combination of terms below which the deal should not proceed - and get this pre-agreed with whoever has final authority before the negotiation, not decided live under pressure.
6. Note exactly what the negotiator is authorized to agree to on the spot versus what requires checking back with someone else - an ambiguous authority level leads to either overcommitting or losing momentum by pausing on things that were actually already approved.
7. Prepare the opening ask deliberately calibrated above the target outcome (leaving real room to concede) rather than opening at the actual target, since opening at the true target leaves no room to trade and can look like there's nothing more to negotiate.

## Output format

```
NEGOTIATION PREP BRIEF - [Vendor] - [Contract/renewal] - [Date]

TERM PRIORITIES:
Must-win: ...
Negotiable/tradeable: ...
Nice-to-have: ...

TRADE OPPORTUNITIES: [give up X tier-3 item for Y tier-1 item]

LEVERAGE ASSESSMENT: [buyer's real leverage - honest read, not assumed]

ANTICIPATED VENDOR COUNTERS:
[Our ask] → Likely counter: ... → Our response: ...

WALK-AWAY POINT (pre-approved by: [name]): ...
NEGOTIATOR'S ON-THE-SPOT AUTHORITY: can agree to [x] alone; must check back on [y]

OPENING ASK (calibrated above target, with room to concede): ...
TARGET OUTCOME: ...
```

## Do
- Rank every contract term into must-win / negotiable / nice-to-have before the negotiation starts
- Assess real leverage honestly rather than assuming a strong position that isn't actually there
- Pre-agree the walk-away point and on-the-spot authority level before entering the room

## Don't
- Don't treat every clause as equally important - that leads to folding on real priorities or fighting over trivial ones
- Don't open at the true target outcome with no room left to concede
- Don't leave the negotiator's authority level ambiguous going into the discussion

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present - walk-away point and authority level are pre-approved, not decided live
- [ ] Terms are explicitly tiered (must-win/negotiable/nice-to-have), not treated as equally weighted
- [ ] Leverage assessment is realistic, not aspirational
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /contract-negotiation-prep-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

---
name: purchase-justification-memo
description: >
  Writes the internal business-case memo justifying a purchase to whoever approves it - cost, alternatives considered, and consequence of not buying - built to survive real budget scrutiny. Triggers: "write the justification memo for this software purchase", "help me make the case to buy this to my manager", /purchase-justification-memo.
metadata:
  category: procurement-vendor-evaluation
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /purchase-justification-memo

Writes the internal memo justifying a purchase to a budget approver - built around real cost, genuine alternatives considered, and the actual consequence of not buying, since generic "this will help us be more efficient" memos get bounced back with follow-up questions.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Whenever a purchase needs sign-off above the requester's own authority - a new tool, a service contract, equipment - and a one-paragraph informal ask risks getting stuck in back-and-forth questions instead of a clean approval.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What's being purchased, the cost (one-time and/or recurring), and who the approver is
- The actual problem this solves or opportunity it captures - specific and concrete, not "improves productivity"
- What alternatives were considered (including doing nothing, or a cheaper/free option) and why this specific option won
- Budget context - is this already budgeted, does it require a budget exception, and what's the approval threshold/process at this organization

## Workflow

1. State the specific problem or opportunity first, in concrete terms an approver outside the requester's day-to-day would understand - "our current process requires 6 manual hours per week reconciling X" beats "this will improve our workflow."
2. Present the actual cost clearly, including anything easy to omit - implementation time, training, any required add-ons, and the ongoing recurring cost, not just the sticker price, since approvers who discover hidden costs later distrust the next request.
3. Show genuine alternatives considered, including the "do nothing" option and at least one cheaper alternative if one exists - a memo that presents only one option looks like the decision was already made and the memo is just theater; showing real alternatives (and why they were rejected) is what makes the ask credible.
4. Quantify the expected benefit as concretely as the data allows - time saved, cost avoided, revenue enabled, risk reduced - and be honest about uncertainty (a range or an estimate basis) rather than presenting a speculative number as a precise fact.
5. State the consequence of NOT approving this purchase specifically - what continues to happen, what risk persists, what opportunity is lost - since approvers weigh the cost of action against the cost of inaction, and the latter is often left unstated.
6. Address budget fit directly: is this within an existing approved budget line, does it require reallocation, or is it a net-new ask - approvers need this context to know what kind of decision they're actually making.
7. End with a specific, easy approval action (a signature, a reply confirming, a specific next step) rather than an open-ended "let me know your thoughts."

## Output format

```
PURCHASE JUSTIFICATION MEMO - [What's being purchased] - [Date]
To: [Approver] | From: [Requester] | Amount: [cost, one-time + recurring]

PROBLEM/OPPORTUNITY: ...

ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED:
1. Do nothing - Why rejected: ...
2. [Cheaper alternative, if any] - Why rejected: ...
3. [Recommended option] - Why chosen: ...

COST BREAKDOWN:
One-time: ... | Recurring: ... | Implementation/training: ...

EXPECTED BENEFIT (quantified where possible): ...
Basis/confidence: [firm estimate / rough estimate - explain]

CONSEQUENCE OF NOT APPROVING: ...

BUDGET FIT: [existing line / reallocation needed / net-new ask]

REQUESTED ACTION: [specific approval step, by when]
```

## Do
- Show genuine alternatives considered, including doing nothing and any cheaper option
- Include the full cost picture - implementation, training, recurring - not just the headline price
- State the consequence of not approving as concretely as the benefit of approving

## Don't
- Don't present only one option as if the decision were already made
- Don't hide recurring costs or add-on requirements behind a low one-time sticker price
- Don't quantify benefits with false precision - mark rough estimates as rough

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Real alternatives (including "do nothing") are shown, not a single predetermined option
- [ ] Full cost picture is disclosed, not just headline price
- [ ] Memo ends with one specific, actionable approval request
```

name: renewal-vs-switch-decision-memo
description: >
  Builds the decision memo weighing renewing an existing vendor against switching to an alternative, accounting for real switching costs that a pure feature/price comparison misses. Triggers: "should we renew or switch vendors for this contract", "help us decide whether to switch from [vendor]", /renewal-vs-switch-decision-memo.
metadata:
  category: procurement-vendor-evaluation
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand

# /renewal-vs-switch-decision-memo

Builds the renew-or-switch decision memo weighing an incumbent vendor against alternatives - the key distinction from a fresh vendor comparison is that switching costs (migration, retraining, risk of disruption) are real, often-underestimated factors that a simple feature/price table misses entirely.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Ahead of a contract renewal deadline when a competing option looks attractive on paper (better price, better features) and the team needs to decide honestly whether switching is actually worth it once real transition costs and risks are counted, not just compared side by side as if starting from zero.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Incumbent vendor's current terms, actual performance/satisfaction over the contract period, and their renewal offer
- The alternative vendor(s) being considered and their offer, plus how confident the team actually is in that vendor's claims (vendor demos and references are optimistic by nature)
- Real switching costs: data migration effort, retraining time, integration rebuilding, any contractual exit costs/penalties with the incumbent, and productivity dip during transition
- How replaceable/critical the incumbent's service is operationally - a low-stakes tool switch is a very different decision than switching a system core operations depend on

## Workflow

1. Start with the incumbent's actual track record over the current contract period (real performance data, SLA history, satisfaction) as the baseline - this is known, verified information, unlike a competitor's promises, and should be weighted accordingly.
2. Price out the alternative honestly including implementation costs, not just the headline subscription/contract price - a cheaper sticker price that requires months of migration and parallel-running costs may be more expensive overall in year one.
3. Quantify switching costs explicitly as their own line items: data migration effort (hours/cost), retraining time, integration/workflow rebuilding, any contractual exit penalty with the incumbent, and a realistic estimate of temporary productivity loss during the transition period - these are commonly ignored in a simple price comparison and are often where "obviously better deal" decisions go wrong.
4. Discount the alternative vendor's claims for unproven risk - a new vendor's promised performance/support quality is unverified until lived with, while the incumbent's flaws are already fully known; weigh a known, moderate problem against an unknown, unverified promise honestly rather than assuming the new vendor will perform exactly as pitched.
5. Assess how business-critical the switch is - if this system/service is deeply embedded in daily operations, the disruption risk during transition should weigh more heavily than for a peripheral, easily-reversible tool.
6. Calculate a rough total cost of ownership for both paths over a realistic time horizon (e.g., 2-3 years) including one-time switching costs amortized in, not just comparing annual contract price side by side.
7. Give a clear recommendation with the specific threshold that would flip the decision (e.g., "switching wins if migration takes under 6 weeks; renew if it's likely to take longer") so the decision-maker understands what assumption the recommendation actually rests on.

## Output format

```
RENEW VS. SWITCH DECISION MEMO - [Current vendor] vs. [Alternative] - Decision needed by: [date]

INCUMBENT TRACK RECORD: [real performance/satisfaction data over current term]
INCUMBENT RENEWAL OFFER: [terms/price]

ALTERNATIVE OFFER: [terms/price] - Confidence in vendor's claims: [verified/unverified, and why]

SWITCHING COSTS (explicit line items):
Migration effort: ... | Retraining: ... | Integration rebuild: ... | Incumbent exit penalty: ... | Estimated productivity dip: ...

CRITICALITY OF THIS SERVICE: [low/medium/high - disruption risk if switch goes poorly]

TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP (over [N] years, switching costs amortized):
Renew: ... | Switch: ...

RECOMMENDATION: [renew/switch] - Key assumption this rests on: ...
```

## Do
- Weight the incumbent's known track record against the alternative's unproven, vendor-claimed performance
- Quantify switching costs as explicit line items, not an afterthought footnote
- Name the specific assumption or threshold the final recommendation actually depends on

## Don't
- Don't compare headline contract prices without folding in real migration/transition costs
- Don't treat a new vendor's demo/reference performance as equally certain to the incumbent's known real-world track record
- Don't recommend switching a business-critical system without weighing disruption risk explicitly

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Switching costs are itemized explicitly, not folded silently into a single price comparison
- [ ] The alternative vendor's unverified claims are discounted relative to the incumbent's known track record
- [ ] Recommendation states the specific assumption/threshold it depends on
```

3. Quantify switching costs explicitly as their own line items: data migration effort (hours/cost), retraining time, integration/workflow rebuilding, any contractual exit penalty with the incumbent, and a realistic estimate of temporary productivity loss during the transition period - these are commonly ignored in a simple price comparison and are often where "obviously better deal" decisions go wrong.
4. Discount the alternative vendor's claims for unproven risk - a new vendor's promised performance/support quality is unverified until lived with, while the incumbent's flaws are already fully known; weigh a known, moderate problem against an unknown, unverified promise honestly rather than assuming the new vendor will perform exactly as pitched.
5. Assess how business-critical the switch is - if this system/service is deeply embedded in daily operations, the disruption risk during transition should weigh more heavily than for a peripheral, easily-reversible tool.
6. Calculate a rough total cost of ownership for both paths over a realistic time horizon (e.g., 2-3 years) including one-time switching costs amortized in, not just comparing annual contract price side by side.
7. Give a clear recommendation with the specific threshold that would flip the decision (e.g., "switching wins if migration takes under 6 weeks; renew if it's likely to take longer") so the decision-maker understands what assumption the recommendation actually rests on.

## Output format

```
RENEW VS. SWITCH DECISION MEMO - [Current vendor] vs. [Alternative] - Decision needed by: [date]

INCUMBENT TRACK RECORD: [real performance/satisfaction data over current term]
INCUMBENT RENEWAL OFFER: [terms/price]

ALTERNATIVE OFFER: [terms/price] - Confidence in vendor's claims: [verified/unverified, and why]

SWITCHING COSTS (explicit line items):
Migration effort: ... | Retraining: ... | Integration rebuild: ... | Incumbent exit penalty: ... | Estimated productivity dip: ...

CRITICALITY OF THIS SERVICE: [low/medium/high - disruption risk if switch goes poorly]

TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP (over [N] years, switching costs amortized):
Renew: ... | Switch: ...

RECOMMENDATION: [renew/switch] - Key assumption this rests on: ...
```

## Do
- Weight the incumbent's known track record against the alternative's unproven, vendor-claimed performance
- Quantify switching costs as explicit line items, not an afterthought footnote
- Name the specific assumption or threshold the final recommendation actually depends on

## Don't
- Don't compare headline contract prices without folding in real migration/transition costs
- Don't treat a new vendor's demo/reference performance as equally certain to the incumbent's known real-world track record
- Don't recommend switching a business-critical system without weighing disruption risk explicitly

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Switching costs are itemized explicitly, not folded silently into a single price comparison
- [ ] The alternative vendor's unverified claims are discounted relative to the incumbent's known track record
- [ ] Recommendation states the specific assumption/threshold it depends on

# /rfp-response-outline

Builds the outline and win-theme strategy for responding to a specific RFP - mapped directly to the buyer's stated evaluation criteria, since RFP responses are scored against a rubric, not read as a general capabilities pitch.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

After receiving an RFP document and before drafting the full response - when the team needs a structural plan and win strategy first, so writing time goes toward the sections that actually score points rather than a generic company overview.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The actual RFP document/requirements, including any stated evaluation criteria or scoring weights (if the RFP doesn't publish weights, note that and make a reasonable inference from section emphasis/length)
- What's genuinely differentiated about this company's offer for this specific buyer (price, a unique capability, relevant past project, faster implementation) - the win theme has to be real, not generic "we're the best" claims
- Known competitors likely also responding, if known, and how this response should position against them
- Any mandatory compliance/format requirements (page limits, required certifications, specific forms) that must be satisfied or the response gets disqualified regardless of content quality

## Workflow

1. Read the RFP's evaluation criteria first (stated or inferable) and build the outline's structure and emphasis around that scoring rubric - sections the buyer weights heavily need the most developed, specific answers; don't spend equal effort on every section by default.
2. Identify and lock in one genuine win theme - the specific, real reason this vendor is the right choice for this buyer - and make sure it's reinforced (not just repeated as a slogan) across every major section, since RFP evaluators read many generic responses and a coherent specific theme stands out.
3. For every mandatory compliance/format requirement (certifications, page limits, required forms, specific section headers), build in an explicit compliance checklist separate from the content outline - a technically excellent response that misses a mandatory requirement can be disqualified before it's even scored on content.
4. In the "past performance/case study" section, select the most relevant proof point(s) for this specific buyer's situation, not just the most impressive project overall - relevance to the buyer's actual use case scores better than raw prestige.
5. In the pricing/commercial section, structure it for scannability the way the evaluator will actually score it (a comparison against RFP-defined scope items) rather than a generic pricing sheet - make it easy for a scorer to check the box that pricing addresses everything asked.
6. Address any obvious weakness or gap the vendor has relative to likely competitors head-on with a mitigation, rather than hoping the evaluator won't notice - evaluators comparing multiple responses tend to notice gaps, and an unaddressed one reads worse than an acknowledged one with a plan.
7. Build in a compliance matrix/traceability table mapping each RFP requirement to the specific section of the response answering it, if the RFP is large or formal - this makes the evaluator's job easier and is often itself a scored or expected element.

## Output format

```
RFP RESPONSE OUTLINE - [RFP name/buyer] - Due: [date]

EVALUATION CRITERIA (stated or inferred) & response emphasis:
[Criterion] - Weight/emphasis: ... - Section(s) addressing it: ...

WIN THEME: [one sentence, real and specific to this buyer]
Reinforced in sections: ...

SECTION-BY-SECTION OUTLINE:
1. Executive summary - [key point to hit]
2. [Technical/solution section] - [key point, mapped to criteria]
3. Past performance/case studies - [most relevant proof point(s) selected]
4. Pricing/commercial - [structured against RFP scope items]
5. Compliance/certifications - [status of each mandatory item]
...

COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST (mandatory format/certification requirements):
[ ] ...

KNOWN WEAKNESS(ES) & MITIGATION:
- ...

REQUIREMENT-TO-SECTION TRACEABILITY (if RFP is large/formal):
[RFP requirement #] → [Response section]
```

## Do
- Structure emphasis around the RFP's actual evaluation criteria/weights, not equal effort everywhere
- Lock a single genuine win theme and reinforce it across sections
- Address known weaknesses head-on with a mitigation rather than hoping they go unnoticed

## Don't
- Don't submit a generic capabilities pitch structure disconnected from the stated evaluation criteria
- Don't skip the mandatory compliance/format checklist - missing one can disqualify an otherwise strong response
- Don't select the most impressive past project over the most relevant one for this specific buyer

- [ ] Outline structure is mapped to the RFP's actual evaluation criteria
- [ ] A specific, real win theme is identified and reinforced across sections
- [ ] Mandatory compliance/format requirements have an explicit checklist
```

# /sla-review-template

Compares a vendor's actual delivered performance against their contracted SLA commitments and produces a scorecard flagging breaches, near-misses, and whether contractual penalty/credit clauses apply - grounded in the real contract language, not a general satisfaction check-in.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

At a regular contractual review cadence (often quarterly) for vendors with defined SLAs, or immediately after a specific incident (an outage, a missed delivery) when the team needs to determine whether it counts as a formal SLA breach with contractual consequences.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The actual SLA terms from the contract (uptime %, response time commitments, delivery windows, quality thresholds) - work from the real contract language, not a general impression of what was promised
- Actual performance data for the review period (uptime logs, support ticket response times, delivery records, defect rates) - flag any metric the reviewer doesn't have access to rather than estimating it
- Whether the contract includes specific remedies for breach (service credits, penalty clauses, termination rights) and their exact trigger conditions
- Any mitigating context worth noting (a mutually agreed exception, a force majeure event, a buyer-caused delay) that affects whether a miss should count as a true breach

## Workflow

1. List each SLA metric from the actual contract individually rather than reviewing performance in the abstract - "99.9% uptime," "4-hour critical response time," "5-day delivery window" are each separately measurable and should each get their own line, not a blended overall impression.
2. Compare actual performance data against each specific metric's threshold, calculating the real number (e.g., actual uptime was 99.4% against a 99.9% commitment) rather than a qualitative "mostly fine" judgment.
3. Distinguish clear breaches (missed the contractual threshold) from near-misses (technically met but with a shrinking margin, worth watching) - near-misses are valuable to flag even without contractual consequence, since they often predict a future breach.
4. Check each breach against the contract's actual remedy clause and trigger conditions - some SLA breaches require the buyer to formally notify within a specific window to claim a credit, so note any time-sensitive action needed to actually exercise a contractual right, not just note that a breach occurred.
5. Apply any genuine mitigating context (force majeure, a buyer-side delay that caused the miss, a pre-agreed exception) honestly - but don't let vendors' informal excuses substitute for contractually defined exceptions unless the contract actually provides for them.
6. Look for a pattern across metrics or across review periods - a single missed metric might be noise, but a metric trending downward over consecutive review periods signals a real, worsening problem worth escalating regardless of whether any single period crossed the contractual line.
7. End with a specific recommended action - invoke a service credit, request a corrective action plan from the vendor, escalate to a renewal-vs-switch evaluation, or simply continue monitoring - rather than ending on the scorecard numbers alone.

## Output format

```
SLA REVIEW - [Vendor name] - Period: [dates]

| SLA Metric | Commitment | Actual | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [e.g., Uptime] | 99.9% | 99.4% | BREACH | ... |
| [e.g., Response time] | 4hrs | 3.5hrs (trending up) | NEAR-MISS | ... |

BREACHES REQUIRING ACTION:
[Metric] - Contractual remedy: ... - Action needed by: [date, if time-sensitive] - Notification requirement: ...

MITIGATING CONTEXT CONSIDERED: ...

TREND ACROSS PERIODS (if prior data available): ...

RECOMMENDED ACTION: [invoke credit / request corrective plan / escalate to renewal-vs-switch review / continue monitoring]
```

## Do
- Work from the actual contracted SLA thresholds and real performance numbers, not general impressions
- Flag near-misses even when no contractual breach technically occurred
- Check and note any time-sensitive notification requirement needed to exercise a contractual remedy

## Don't
- Don't blend multiple SLA metrics into one vague overall satisfaction score
- Don't accept a vendor's informal excuse as an exception unless the contract actually defines that exception
- Don't end the review without a specific recommended next action

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Each SLA metric is measured individually against its actual contracted threshold
- [ ] Any time-sensitive step needed to claim a contractual remedy is flagged
- [ ] A specific recommended action follows the scorecard, not just raw numbers
```

# /supplier-risk-checklist

Produces a supplier risk assessment across the categories that actually cause supply chain and vendor failures - financial instability, operational single points of failure, compliance exposure, and concentration risk - with a specific rating and mitigation plan, not a generic due-diligence box-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before onboarding a new critical supplier, before a major contract renewal with an existing supplier, or periodically for suppliers the business has significant dependency on - most valuable when the cost of supplier failure (missed delivery, data breach, financial collapse) would materially hurt the business.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What this supplier provides and how critical/replaceable it is to operations (a single-source critical component supplier carries very different risk than an easily-swapped commodity vendor)
- Available information: financial statements/credit report if accessible, years in business, client references, any public news (litigation, layoffs, leadership changes), certifications (SOC 2, ISO, industry-specific)
- What percentage of the supplier's own business this contract represents, if knowable - a supplier where the buyer is a huge % of their revenue carries different risk than one where the buyer is a small account
- Any existing incidents or yellow flags already noticed (late deliveries, quality issues, unresponsive support) that prompted this assessment

## Safety

- Propose before acting: present the risk rating and any recommended mitigation/hold before it drives a purchasing decision - a risk assessment should inform, not silently gate, a business decision that may have other overriding factors.
- Flag clearly when a risk category could not be assessed due to missing information (e.g., "financial risk: unrated - no financial data available") rather than defaulting to a falsely reassuring "low risk" absent real evidence.

## Workflow

1. Assess financial stability using whatever real evidence is available (credit report, public filings, years in business, funding history) - if no financial data is available, say so explicitly and treat that as an open risk rather than assuming stability.
2. Assess operational/single-point-of-failure risk: does this supplier have backup capacity, multiple facilities/locations, or is there a real single point where one disruption (a fire, a key departure, a single facility outage) takes down the whole supply - this is often the most underrated risk category since it's invisible until something actually breaks.
3. Assess concentration risk on both sides - how replaceable is this supplier for the buyer (sole-source vs. multiple alternatives), and how significant is the buyer to the supplier's own revenue (a supplier deeply dependent on this one contract may cut corners elsewhere or be unstable if the buyer ever reduces orders).
4. Assess compliance/security exposure relevant to what's being purchased - data handling certifications if data is involved, safety/quality certifications if physical goods, labor/ethical sourcing standards if relevant to the buyer's own compliance obligations - and flag any gap rather than assuming compliance without evidence.
5. Weight the categories by what's actually relevant to this specific purchase - a risk assessment for a software data-processing vendor should weight compliance/security heavily; one for a physical parts supplier should weight operational/single-point-of-failure risk heavily.
6. Assign an overall risk rating (e.g., Low/Medium/High) with the specific reasoning driving it, and don't let a strong score in one category silently offset a serious flag in another - a financially excellent but single-source, uncertified supplier for a critical, regulated input is still high risk overall.
7. For every flagged risk, propose a specific mitigation (dual-sourcing a backup, requiring a specific certification before signing, a contractual SLA/penalty clause, a shorter initial contract term to observe performance) rather than leaving the rating as an unresolved warning with no path forward.

## Output format

```
SUPPLIER RISK ASSESSMENT - [Supplier name] - [What's being purchased] - [Date]

FINANCIAL STABILITY: [Low/Medium/High/Unrated] - Evidence: ...
OPERATIONAL RISK (single points of failure): [Low/Medium/High] - Evidence: ...

# /supplier-risk-checklist

Produces a supplier risk assessment across the categories that actually cause supply chain and vendor failures - financial instability, operational single points of failure, compliance exposure, and concentration risk - with a specific rating and mitigation plan, not a generic due-diligence box-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before onboarding a new critical supplier, before a major contract renewal with an existing supplier, or periodically for suppliers the business has significant dependency on - most valuable when the cost of supplier failure (missed delivery, data breach, financial collapse) would materially hurt the business.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What this supplier provides and how critical/replaceable it is to operations (a single-source critical component supplier carries very different risk than an easily-swapped commodity vendor)
- Available information: financial statements/credit report if accessible, years in business, client references, any public news (litigation, layoffs, leadership changes), certifications (SOC 2, ISO, industry-specific)
- What percentage of the supplier's own business this contract represents, if knowable - a supplier where the buyer is a huge % of their revenue carries different risk than one where the buyer is a small account
- Any existing incidents or yellow flags already noticed (late deliveries, quality issues, unresponsive support) that prompted this assessment

## Safety

- Propose before acting: present the risk rating and any recommended mitigation/hold before it drives a purchasing decision - a risk assessment should inform, not silently gate, a business decision that may have other overriding factors.
- Flag clearly when a risk category could not be assessed due to missing information (e.g., "financial risk: unrated - no financial data available") rather than defaulting to a falsely reassuring "low risk" absent real evidence.

## Workflow

1. Assess financial stability using whatever real evidence is available (credit report, public filings, years in business, funding history) - if no financial data is available, say so explicitly and treat that as an open risk rather than assuming stability.
2. Assess operational/single-point-of-failure risk: does this supplier have backup capacity, multiple facilities/locations, or is there a real single point where one disruption (a fire, a key departure, a single facility outage) takes down the whole supply - this is often the most underrated risk category since it's invisible until something actually breaks.
3. Assess concentration risk on both sides - how replaceable is this supplier for the buyer (sole-source vs. multiple alternatives), and how significant is the buyer to the supplier's own revenue (a supplier deeply dependent on this one contract may cut corners elsewhere or be unstable if the buyer ever reduces orders).
4. Assess compliance/security exposure relevant to what's being purchased - data handling certifications if data is involved, safety/quality certifications if physical goods, labor/ethical sourcing standards if relevant to the buyer's own compliance obligations - and flag any gap rather than assuming compliance without evidence.
5. Weight the categories by what's actually relevant to this specific purchase - a risk assessment for a software data-processing vendor should weight compliance/security heavily; one for a physical parts supplier should weight operational/single-point-of-failure risk heavily.
6. Assign an overall risk rating (e.g., Low/Medium/High) with the specific reasoning driving it, and don't let a strong score in one category silently offset a serious flag in another - a financially excellent but single-source, uncertified supplier for a critical, regulated input is still high risk overall.
7. For every flagged risk, propose a specific mitigation (dual-sourcing a backup, requiring a specific certification before signing, a contractual SLA/penalty clause, a shorter initial contract term to observe performance) rather than leaving the rating as an unresolved warning with no path forward.

```

FINANCIAL STABILITY: [Low/Medium/High/Unrated] - Evidence: ...
OPERATIONAL RISK (single points of failure): [Low/Medium/High] - Evidence: ...
CONCENTRATION RISK: Buyer's alternatives: [many/few/sole-source] - Supplier's dependency on this contract: [low/high/unknown]
COMPLIANCE/SECURITY: [Low/Medium/High/Unrated] - Certifications held: ... - Gaps: ...

OVERALL RISK RATING: [Low/Medium/High] - Reasoning (not a simple average): ...

MITIGATIONS RECOMMENDED:
- [Risk flagged] → [Specific mitigation]
...

DATA GAPS (unratable categories): ...
```

## Do
- Rate financial/compliance categories as "unrated" when no real evidence exists, rather than defaulting to a falsely reassuring low-risk score
- Let a serious single-category flag drive up the overall rating even if other categories score well
- Propose a specific, concrete mitigation for every flagged risk

## Don't
- Don't assume compliance or financial stability without actual evidence
- Don't average risk categories into a misleadingly moderate overall score when one category is genuinely severe
- Don't let this assessment silently gate a purchasing decision - surface it for explicit review

# /vendor-comparison-matrix

Builds a weighted vendor comparison matrix scored against the buyer's actual priorities - not a neutral feature checklist, but a decision tool that produces a defensible ranked recommendation.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When evaluating 2 or more vendor/supplier options for a purchase decision and the buyer needs a structured, defensible comparison to present internally - especially useful when stakeholders have different informal preferences and the decision needs to be grounded in agreed criteria rather than whoever argues loudest.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The vendors being compared and whatever information is available on each (pricing, features, references, contract terms) - flag any gaps rather than filling them with assumptions
- The buyer's actual decision criteria and their relative importance (cost, feature fit, support quality, implementation time, vendor stability/size) - ask what matters most if this isn't already clear, since a matrix built on generic criteria produces a generic (and often wrong) recommendation
- Any hard disqualifiers (must integrate with X, must be SOC 2 compliant, must support a specific region) that should filter vendors before scoring rather than just lowering their score
- Whether this is for a new purchase or a renewal-vs-switch decision, since the switching cost itself becomes a real criterion in the latter case

## Workflow

1. Apply hard disqualifiers first, before any scoring - if a vendor fails a genuine must-have (compliance requirement, required integration, budget ceiling), remove it from the scored comparison rather than including it with a low score, since that clutters the matrix with options that were never actually viable.
2. Establish weighted criteria with the buyer's actual priorities, not a generic template - ask which 4-6 factors matter most and roughly how they should be weighted (e.g., cost 25%, feature fit 30%, support 20%, implementation time 15%, vendor stability 10%) rather than weighting everything equally by default.
3. Score each surviving vendor against each criterion using a consistent scale (e.g., 1-5) with a one-line justification per score - a bare number with no reasoning can't be defended later if someone challenges the recommendation.
4. Flag any score based on incomplete information explicitly (e.g., "support quality: estimated from public reviews, not a direct reference check") rather than presenting an inferred score with the same confidence as a verified one.
5. Calculate the weighted total per vendor and rank them, but don't stop there - write a short qualitative override note if the top-scoring vendor has a significant risk the numeric score doesn't capture (e.g., a startup with financial instability scoring well on features) since matrices can miss real dealbreakers.
6. Include a genuine runner-up analysis, not just a winner - note what would need to change for the #2 vendor to become the better choice, since this is often the most useful information for a close decision.
7. End with a clear recommendation statement in plain language, separate from the matrix itself, that a non-technical stakeholder could read and understand the "why" in one paragraph.

## Output format

```
VENDOR COMPARISON MATRIX - [Decision: what's being purchased]

DISQUALIFIED (failed hard requirements): [Vendor] - Reason: ...

CRITERIA & WEIGHTS:
[Criterion] - Weight: [%]
...

SCORING (1-5 scale):
| Vendor | [Criterion 1] | [Criterion 2] | ... | Weighted Total |
|--------|---------------|---------------|-----|----------------|
| A      | 4 (reason)    | 3 (reason)    | ... | X.X            |

DATA CONFIDENCE FLAGS: [any score based on incomplete/inferred info]

RANKING: 1. ... 2. ... 3. ...
QUALITATIVE OVERRIDE (if any): [risk the numbers don't capture]
RUNNER-UP ANALYSIS: what would change the #2 vendor's ranking

RECOMMENDATION (plain language): ...
```

## Do
- Weight criteria to the buyer's actual stated priorities, never a generic default split
- Justify every individual score with a one-line reason
- Flag scores based on incomplete or inferred data explicitly

## Don't
- Don't include vendors that fail a genuine hard disqualifier in the scored comparison
- Don't present an inferred score with the same confidence as a verified one
- Don't let the numeric ranking be the final word if a real qualitative risk isn't captured by the criteria

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Criteria weights reflect this buyer's actual stated priorities, not a generic default
- [ ] Every score includes a one-line justification
- [ ] Any qualitative risk not captured by the numeric score is called out explicitly
```

# /vendor-onboarding-checklist

Produces the operational checklist for bringing a newly contracted vendor/supplier live - access, compliance paperwork, and internal system setup - the procurement-side counterpart to signing a contract, since a signed deal with no onboarding plan often stalls for weeks.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Immediately after a vendor contract is signed, when the business needs the vendor actually working/delivering - not just contracted - and there's a real checklist of internal and vendor-side steps standing between signature and first delivery.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What kind of vendor this is (software/SaaS, professional services, physical goods supplier, staffing/contractor) since onboarding requirements differ significantly by type
- Internal systems needing setup on the buyer's side (accounts payable/vendor master record, procurement system entry, access provisioning if it's a service provider needing system access)
- Compliance documentation required before the vendor can be paid or start work (W-9/tax forms, insurance certificates, security questionnaire, signed NDA/DPA) per this organization's actual policy
- Who owns vendor onboarding internally (procurement, IT, finance, the requesting department) since these steps typically span multiple internal teams

## Workflow

1. Separate the checklist into internal buyer-side setup (vendor master record/AP setup, procurement system entry, budget code assignment) and vendor-side requirements (tax forms, insurance certificates, security questionnaires, signed data agreements) since these move in parallel with different owners.
2. Identify any step that blocks first payment or first delivery specifically (e.g., "vendor cannot be paid until W-9 and vendor master record are both complete") and flag these as critical-path items distinct from steps that can happen in parallel or slightly after.
3. If the vendor needs system/data access (common for software vendors or service providers working inside internal tools), route this through the buyer's actual access-provisioning process explicitly, with least-privilege access as the default rather than broad access granted for convenience.
4. For vendors handling any sensitive data or requiring security review, confirm the security/compliance questionnaire is complete and any required certifications (SOC 2, ISO, industry-specific) are on file before granting access or starting data-sharing - flag this as a hold point if it's not yet done.
5. Assign a specific internal owner and target date to every single item - cross-functional onboarding checklists routinely stall because no one function believes it owns a given step.
6. Include a specific first-deliverable or first-invoice checkpoint date, so if onboarding is dragging past a reasonable window, someone is prompted to intervene rather than letting it silently slip.
7. Note any onboarding step that differs from this organization's standard process because of something specific about this vendor (e.g., an international vendor with different tax documentation, a sole proprietor without standard insurance) so exceptions are documented, not just handled ad hoc.

## Output format

```
VENDOR ONBOARDING CHECKLIST - [Vendor name] - Contract signed: [date]

BLOCKS FIRST PAYMENT/DELIVERY:
[ ] [Item] - Owner: ... - Target date: ...

INTERNAL BUYER-SIDE SETUP:
[ ] Vendor master record / AP setup - Owner: ...
[ ] Procurement system entry - Owner: ...
[ ] Budget code assignment - Owner: ...

VENDOR-SIDE COMPLIANCE DOCS:
[ ] Tax form (W-9/equivalent) - Owner: ...
[ ] Insurance certificate - Owner: ...
[ ] Security questionnaire / certifications on file - Owner: ...
[ ] Signed NDA/DPA - Owner: ...

ACCESS PROVISIONING (if applicable): [systems, least-privilege scope, owner]

TARGET: first deliverable/invoice by [date]

EXCEPTIONS TO STANDARD PROCESS: ...
```

## Do
- Flag which specific items block first payment or first delivery, distinct from parallel-track items
- Assign a named internal owner to every checklist item
- Default to least-privilege access for any vendor needing system/data access

## Don't
- Don't grant broad system access for convenience before the security review is complete
- Don't leave cross-functional items unowned - that's the most common reason onboarding stalls
- Don't skip documenting exceptions when this vendor's onboarding differs from standard process

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Critical-path items blocking payment/delivery are explicitly flagged
- [ ] Every item has a named internal owner
- [ ] Security/compliance documentation is confirmed before access is granted
```
